home.closingDesc: Open a real verified photo. Or find out why this matters now.

technology.heroSubtitle: C2PA provides the cryptographic foundation. Title Protocol makes it survive the real world.
technology.leadIn: C2PA, the open standard backed by Adobe, Microsoft, and Google, lets cameras sign content at capture. The technology works. The problem is what happens next.
technology.closingDesc: Open a verified photo and inspect the proof yourself. Everything on the verification page runs in your browser.

why.heroSubtitle: Digital content has lost its ability to prove where it came from, who made it, and whether it has been altered. This affects every industry that depends on visual evidence.
why.closingDesc: Title Protocol separates the proof from the file, so verification survives even when platforms strip the metadata.

developers.heroSubtitle: The protocol, tools, and documentation are open source. Run a node, build an application, or contribute to the specification.
developers.involved1Text: Verify content and earn fees. The node software is open source and runs on standard hardware.
developers.involved2Text: Title Protocol is not just for photos. Any C2PA-signed content can be registered. Build verification into your own product.
developers.involved3Text: The full protocol specification covers the trust model, data structures, TEE requirements, and extension architecture.
developers.closingDesc: Everything you need is on GitHub.

about.heroSubtitle: A camera app that proves your content is real, built on an open protocol designed to outlast any single product.
about.flowIntro: Here is what happens under the hood when you publish a photo:
about.closingDesc: Try a verified photo, explore the code, or read the spec.

hero.title: Prove it's real.
hero.tagline: Take a photo. Get a tamper-proof record that it was really captured. Share a link anyone can check.
hero.description: RootLens is a camera app that attaches a digital signature the moment you take a photo. Title Protocol saves that proof as a permanent, independent record. Even when social media strips the data from your file, the proof still exists.

appFlow.title: How it works

step1.label: Capture
step1.text: Take a photo or video with RootLens. A digital signature is attached instantly.

step2.label: Prove
step2.text: Title Protocol verifies the signature and saves the proof as a permanent record, separate from the file itself.

step3.label: Share
step3.text: Get a link. Post it anywhere. Anyone who opens it can see when, where, and on what device the content was captured.

appFlow.editing: You can still crop, mask, or trim before sharing. RootLens tracks every edit, so viewers can see exactly what was changed and confirm the original is real.

issues.title: Why this matters
issues.intro: Across industries, the same crisis is unfolding: nobody can tell what's real anymore.

sns.label: Social media & creators
sns.text: Deepfake videos go viral with fabricated news logos. Creators face accusations that real content is AI-generated. Unauthorized reposting is the norm, and revenue goes to the copier, not the original creator.

media.label: News & journalism
media.text: Reporters face accusations that real footage was AI-generated. Video evidence, once considered conclusive, is now questioned by default. The speed of misinformation structurally outpaces correction.

insurance.label: Insurance & finance
insurance.text: An estimated 10% of insurance claims involve fraud. Damage photos, medical documents, and property listings can be fabricated with increasing sophistication. Recipients have no independent way to verify authenticity.

ai.label: AI, courts, healthcare, academia
ai.text: AI companies face billion-dollar lawsuits over training data rights. Forged documents challenge court evidence. Telemedicine images lack tamper verification. Research retractions rise as image fabrication becomes trivial.

c2pa.title: The right foundation already exists
c2pa.p1: The root cause of all these problems is the same: digital content's origin and rightful owner are unknowable.
c2pa.p2: Detecting fakes after the fact, using AI or watermarks, is a structural arms race. As generation improves, so does the ability to fake. There is no inherent advantage for defenders.
c2pa.p3: The sound approach is to record provenance at the time of creation with cryptographic signatures. The security of these signatures depends on the integrity of signing keys, the certificate chain, and the implementation. But unlike post-hoc detection, this approach has a structural foundation rather than an endless game of catch-up.
c2pa.p4: C2PA (Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity), backed by Adobe, Microsoft, Google, and over 6,000 organizations, is the open standard for exactly this. It records who captured content, when, with what tool, and what edits were made, all cryptographically bound to the content.
c2pa.p5: Google Pixel signs photos with its Titan M2 chip. Sony, Nikon, and Canon cameras embed C2PA signatures. OpenAI, Google, and Adobe sign their AI-generated content with C2PA. The infrastructure is real and growing.

gap.title: But proof dies when shared
gap.p1: C2PA verification requires two things: the full manifest data (a JUMBF container holding the signature chain, certificates, and edit history) and the complete original binary of the content itself. For a photo, that means the full-resolution capture. For a video, the entire original file. The cryptographic hash must match byte-for-byte. This tight coupling is how C2PA guarantees integrity.
gap.p2: The problem: nearly every social media platform, messaging app, and CDN strips the manifest and recompresses the content on upload. Facebook, Instagram, X, and YouTube all remove C2PA data and alter the binary. Once that happens, verification becomes impossible. The World Privacy Forum calls this "the primary obstacle to C2PA interoperability."
gap.p3: Even when C2PA data survives, the verified attributes inside (hardware capture proof, timestamp, edit history) can only be accessed by parsing the full manifest together with the original binary. There is no way for a third party to independently query those attributes without having the complete original file.
gap.p4: Existing solutions try to recover the full manifest through watermarks or centralized cloud storage. But these approaches either rely on a single company's infrastructure or attempt to reconstruct what was lost, rather than solving the underlying coupling.
gap.p5: To build an app where proof of authenticity actually reaches people on social media, we needed to solve a different problem: separating verified attributes from the content, so the proof remains accessible even without the complete original file.

tp.title: Title Protocol: making proof independent
tp.subtitle: Extracting verified attributes from content and recording them as standalone, trustworthy records.
tp.p1: Title Protocol takes C2PA-signed content and verifies it inside a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE): hardware-isolated processing designed so that the server operator cannot access or alter the verification. The verified attributes are then extracted into independent on-chain records.
tp.p2: The result: provenance data that exists on its own. No original file needed. Anyone can query the attributes (who captured it, when, with what device, whether it was hardware-signed) directly from the blockchain.

step1.label: Sign at capture
step1.text: C2PA records who captured the content, when, and with what tool, cryptographically bound to the content.

step2.label: Verify in isolated hardware
step2.text: A TEE (currently targeting AWS Nitro Enclaves) verifies the C2PA data. The TEE is designed so that the operator cannot access the process. The verification result is signed with the TEE's internal key.

step3.label: Extract and record
step3.text: The verified attributes are extracted and recorded on the Solana blockchain as a compressed NFT. Cost at current network conditions: ~$0.00015 per record. 100,000 records per month cost roughly $15.

step4.label: Anyone can verify, anywhere
step4.text: Your browser fetches data directly from blockchain nodes and checks the TEE signature. The proof exists independently of the content file.

stateless.label: Stateless TEE: two-phase processing
stateless.text: Phase 1 (Verify): the TEE decrypts the content, verifies the C2PA signature chain, and signs the verification result with its own key. The signed result is returned to the client encrypted. Phase 2 (Sign): after the client stores the signed result in permanent off-chain storage (Arweave), the TEE re-verifies its own signature, constructs a blockchain transaction, and partially signs it. The client applies the final wallet signature and broadcasts. This design means the TEE never needs to hold a wallet, never maintains state between requests, and can be restarted or replaced at any time. If a TEE instance is compromised, the damage is temporally localized: it cannot retroactively affect records already on the blockchain.

e2ee.label: Client-side encryption
e2ee.text: The content owner encrypts the content and wallet address on their own device before uploading. Encryption uses an ephemeral X25519 keypair: the client generates a one-time key, derives a shared secret with the TEE's public key (published on-chain in Global Config), and encrypts with AES-256-GCM. The TEE's response is also encrypted with the same derived key. Node operators see only ciphertext and cannot read, modify, or determine the destination wallet. The ephemeral key is discarded after use, providing forward secrecy.

coreExt.label: Core / Extension architecture
coreExt.text: Core records the C2PA provenance chain and rights structure. Extensions add computed attributes (perceptual hash, hardware signing proof, AI detection flags) as WASM modules executed inside the TEE. Extensions enable content lookup even after platforms strip the original metadata.

cost.label: Compressed NFT cost optimization
cost.text: Using Solana's Bubblegum compressed NFT standard, per-record cost is ~¥0.023 (~$0.00015) at current network conditions. 100,000 monthly registrations cost roughly ¥2,250 (~$15).

security.label: Security considerations
security.text: TEEs are not infallible. Side-channel attacks have been demonstrated against some implementations (e.g., Foreshadow against SGX). Title Protocol mitigates this through multiple layers: (1) Stateless operation: no persistent keys survive across requests, so a compromised instance cannot affect past or future records. (2) Wallet key separation: the TEE never holds the user's wallet key; it only partially signs transactions. (3) Client-side encryption: even if the TEE host is compromised, the node operator still cannot read content because encryption happens on the user's device. (4) Remote attestation: clients verify TEE integrity before submitting content. (5) All TEE enclave code is open source and independently auditable. The protocol also explicitly does not prevent provenance laundering (re-registering someone else's content). Similarity-based deduplication at registration time would destroy the low-cost design, so duplicate detection is handled at query time by applications. Content semantics (legality, appropriateness) are also outside protocol scope and are application-layer responsibilities.

comparison.title: What exists today and what remains unsolved
comparison.intro: C2PA verification services and provenance registries exist. Here is where the gaps remain.

scenario1.label: Proof that survives sharing
scenario1.text: C2PA data is stripped by nearly every platform. Existing solutions attempt to recover the full manifest via watermarks or centralized cloud. Title Protocol takes a different approach: verified attributes exist as independent records on the blockchain, so even after C2PA metadata is stripped from the file, the provenance data remains accessible without recovering the original manifest.

scenario2.label: Querying attributes without the file
scenario2.text: Existing services store full C2PA manifests externally, so you still need to parse the manifest to access specific attributes. Title Protocol extracts and records individual verified attributes, making them directly queryable from the blockchain.

scenario3.label: Cross-platform rights tracing
scenario3.text: When content goes viral, tracing the original creator requires searching across fragmented services, each with its own ID scheme. Title Protocol uses a content ID derived from the SHA-256 hash of the C2PA manifest signature. This is a value anyone can compute from the same C2PA content, independent of Title Protocol. Rights holders are linked by wallet address, enabling cross-platform tracing through a neutral, non-proprietary identifier.

scenario4.label: Trusting the verifier
scenario4.text: If a company runs the verification, you must trust that company. Title Protocol uses TEE: the verification is designed to happen in hardware-isolated processing where the operator cannot intervene. The result is cryptographically signed and client-verifiable. All TEE enclave code is open source, so the verification logic itself can be independently audited. The trust shifts from a company's reputation to a publicly auditable codebase and security model.

rootlens.title: RootLens
rootlens.subtitle: The reason we built Title Protocol
rootlens.p1: RootLens is a camera app that proves your photo or video was really captured, and lets you share that proof on any platform.
rootlens.step2: The app signs it with C2PA using the device's secure hardware
rootlens.step3: Publish. Title Protocol extracts and records the proof
rootlens.step4: Share the link anywhere. Anyone can verify it
rootlens.permanence: The proof outlives the app. If RootLens disappears tomorrow, the verification records remain on the blockchain. Other applications built on Title Protocol can access the same records.
rootlens.targets: For creators who face AI-generation accusations, journalists who need verifiable evidence, and anyone whose content is worth proving real.

openSource.title: Open source. Open protocol.
openSource.spec: The specification, architecture documentation, and implementation are all publicly available. You can read the docs and independently verify or reimplement the entire system.
openSource.node: Anyone can run a verification node. Anyone can build an application on the protocol. Governance is managed by a DAO, and no single entity controls it.
openSource.code: All source code is public, released under Apache 2.0.

footer.builtBy: Built by Yudai Mori and Akito Kawano

trust.failed: Some checks could not be confirmed

intro.title: Verification via Title Protocol
intro.desc: Title Protocol is an open protocol that verifies the provenance and authenticity of C2PA-signed content inside a TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) and records the results on the Solana blockchain. For each piece of content, a Core cNFT (provenance record) and any number of Extension cNFTs (attribute verification) are issued.
intro.trustChain: Trust is established through the following chain: Global Config on-chain (managed by DAO) defines the official collection address → confirm the cNFT belongs to the official collection → fetch off-chain data from the URI referenced by the cNFT → verify the TEE signature to confirm data integrity. Each step is independently executed in this browser.

core.title: Core cNFT — Provenance Record
core.desc: The Core cNFT records the result of verifying the C2PA signature chain embedded in the content, signed by the TEE and stored on the blockchain. The entire provenance — who captured it, when, and with what tool — is preserved in a tamper-proof form.
core.collectionPass: Confirmed membership in the official Title Protocol collection
core.collectionFail: Could not confirm membership in the official collection
core.teeSigPass: TEE-generated signature is valid — proves data has not been tampered with
core.c2paFail: Could not confirm provenance chain record
core.offchainDesc: The TEE signs the verification result and returns it to the client (SDK), which then permanently stores it in off-chain storage. The protocol does not mandate a specific storage provider, but Arweave is recommended for Core due to its permanence guarantees. The TEE is stateless by design and never accesses storage directly. You can see the actual storage URI in the "On-chain References" section below.
core.noLinks: None (single content — no ingredient materials used)
core.creator: Registrant Wallet

ext.title: Extension cNFTs — Attribute Verification
ext.desc: Extensions are objective attribute values computed by WASM modules inside the TEE from the raw content data. While Core handles the C2PA provenance chain (rights structure between content), Extensions handle properties of individual content (perceptual hash, hardware signing, etc.). There is no limit on the number of Extensions — new verification items can be freely added through new WASM modules.

phash.title: image-phash — Image Identity Verification
phash.desc: A perceptual hash (pHash) is a numerical fingerprint of an image's visual appearance. A WASM module inside the TEE computes it from the original image and records it on-chain. The viewer's browser recomputes the pHash from the displayed image using the identical WASM binary, then compares it with the on-chain value. If the Hamming distance (bit-level difference) is within the threshold, the images are deemed identical.
phash.matchPass: Hamming distance: {distance} (threshold: {threshold} or less to pass)

hardware.title: {id} — Hardware Capture Proof
hardware.desc: This Extension cryptographically proves that the content was physically captured by the device's secure hardware (e.g., Google Pixel's Titan M2 chip or iOS Secure Enclave). A WASM module verifies the hardware signature within the C2PA signature chain, providing third-party verifiable proof of physical capture.

why.title: Why You Can Trust This Verification
why.trustless: Why trustless verification is possible without the original C2PA data
why.trustlessDesc: The C2PA verification — validating the signature chain and hash-matching against the content body — was already completed inside the TEE. The TEE signed the verification result with its private key (Ed25519), and the client permanently stored it in off-chain storage. Viewers only need to verify this TEE signature; there is no need to possess the original C2PA binary data. If the TEE signature is valid, it confirms that "a trusted execution environment certified that C2PA verification passed."
why.tsaDesc: An RFC 3161-compliant TSA (Time Stamping Authority) is a mechanism where a third-party authority cryptographically certifies the capture time. The TSA timestamp is embedded within the C2PA signature chain, and the TEE verified its validity before recording it. This ensures that no one — including RootLens — can falsify the capture time.
why.noServer: No RootLens server is involved in verification
why.noServerDesc: All data used in this verification is fetched directly from Solana RPC nodes and off-chain storage. The RootLens server serves the page and display images, but has no involvement in the verification process. You can confirm the network destinations in your browser's developer tools (Network tab).
why.oss: All source code is publicly available
why.ossDesc: The source code for both Title Protocol and RootLens is fully published on GitHub. The verification logic running on this page, the protocol specification and implementation, the code executed inside the TEE — you can inspect everything yourself and independently reproduce the verification.

notfound.title: Content not found
notfound.description: This link may be invalid or the content may have been removed.
